claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 14 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you some questions about your child or children.

**First, let me ask about the child's age:**

How old is your child? This is important because eligibility rules differ based on age:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (if they're not working full-time)
- Young people aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those aged 20 or over typically don't qualify

Could you tell me:
1. How old is your child (or children)?
2. If you have multiple children, please let me know all of their ages and names so I can assess eligibility for each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! Yes, I have two children I'd like to ask about. 

My youngest is Alex, who's 8 years old, and then Blake is 14. They both live at home with me here in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have:
- **Alex** who is 8 years old
- **Blake** who is 14 years old

Both are living at home with you in the UK. That's helpful!

Since both Alex (8) and Blake (14) are under 16, let me check the next requirement. For children under 16, I need to verify they're not working full-time.

**Are either Alex or Blake in full-time paid work?** 

(Full-time work means working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. Alex is only 8, so obviously too young for that, and Blake doesn't have a job either - just focuses on school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! That's very clear. Since neither Alex nor Blake are working, they both meet the requirement for children under 16.

Now let me move on to the next important question about **your residency status**.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check a few more things about Alex and Blake:

**Are either Alex or Blake receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This would include things like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed in the child's own name (not as part of your household claim).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name. They're just kids - any benefits we get are in my name for the household.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, that's what I expected for children their age. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, one more question about the children:

**Are either Alex or Blake in an apprenticeship program?**

Given their ages (8 and 14), I expect not, but I need to confirm they're not in any apprenticeship schemes.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not. Alex is only 8, so far too young for that. And Blake at 14 is still just doing regular school - no apprenticeships or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's exactly what I expected. Both children are just in regular school, which is great.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now let's move on to questions about **your** eligibility as the claimant.

You mentioned earlier that you and the children live in the UK. Let me ask about your residency status:

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

This means the UK is your main home where you usually live. If your situation is more complex (for example, if you have pre-settled or settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or you're a Crown servant abroad), please let me know and I can check the specific requirements.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK. This is my main home - I live here with Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That satisfies the residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You've already mentioned they live at home with you, which definitely satisfies this requirement. Responsibility means either:
- Living with the child, OR
- Contributing to their upkeep (paying for their care, food, clothes, etc.)

Since they live with you, you clearly meet this requirement. Can you confirm that's correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct. They both live with me and I look after them - I provide everything they need like food, clothes, school things, all of that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! You clearly meet the responsibility requirement since Alex and Blake live with you and you provide for all their needs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask an important question about other potential claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- An ex-partner
- A grandparent or other family member

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. If someone else is claiming, they would need to stop their claim before you could claim.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them. It's just me looking after them, and no one else has made a claim.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's clear - no one else is claiming for either Alex or Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about the children's living situation. You mentioned they live at home with you, but I need to confirm a few specific circumstances:

**Are either Alex or Blake currently:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally at home with you?

From what you've told me, it sounds like they're living normally at home with you, but I need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're both living normally at home with me. Neither of them are in hospital, care, or being fostered - just a regular home situation with me as their parent.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! They're both living normally at home with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Almost there! Just one final question:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's or Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

This means the council isn't contributing financially to their housing or living costs. Since you've said they live at home with you and you provide for them, I expect the answer is no (the council is not paying), but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance. I'm covering all their costs myself - housing, food, everything they need.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's exactly what I needed to confirm.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Excellent News - You're Eligible!

**You are eligible for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake!**

### Why You Qualify:

✅ **For Alex (8 years old):**
- Under 16 years old (automatic qualification)
- Not in full-time work
- Not receiving benefits in their own right
- Not in an apprenticeship
- Lives with you and you're responsible for them

✅ **For Blake (14 years old):**
- Under 16 years old (automatic qualification)
- Not in full-time work
- Not receiving benefits in their own right
- Not in an apprenticeship
- Lives with you and you're responsible for them

✅ **Your eligibility as the claimant:**
- You normally live in the UK
- You're responsible for both children (living with them and providing for their needs)
- No one else is claiming for either child
- The local council isn't paying towards their costs

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply for Child Benefit** - You can apply:
   - Online at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)
   - Or by post using form CH2

2. **Documents you'll need:**
   - Proof of your identity
   - Birth certificates or adoption certificates for Alex and Blake

3. **Payment details:**
   - Payments are usually made every 4 weeks
   - (Weekly payments available if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

4. **⚠️ Important - High Income Child Benefit Charge:**
   - If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through the High Income Child Benefit Charge
   - You can still claim and choose not to receive payments (to protect National Insurance credits)

Do you have any questions about claiming or the next steps?

reasonings: Alex (8 years old) meets all eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification; (2) Not working full-time (less than 24 hours/week); (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) Lives with the claimant who is responsible for them; (6) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (7) Living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (8) Local council is not paying towards accommodation or maintenance. The claimant also meets residency requirements by normally living in the UK.

Blake (14 years old) meets all eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification; (2) Not working full-time (less than 24 hours/week); (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) Lives with the claimant who is responsible for them; (6) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake; (7) Living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (8) Local council is not paying towards accommodation or maintenance. The claimant also meets residency requirements by normally living in the UK.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children, Alex (8) and Blake (14). Both children meet all the qualifying child requirements as they are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in apprenticeships. The claimant meets all claimant requirements: normally resident in the UK, responsible for both children (living with them and providing for all their needs), no other claimant exists for either child, both children live normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (8 years old) meets all eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification; (2) Not working full-time (less than 24 hours/week); (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) Lives with the claimant who is responsible for them; (6) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (7) Living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (8) Local council is not paying towards accommodation or maintenance. The claimant also meets residency requirements by normally living in the UK.

Blake (14 years old) meets all eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification; (2) Not working full-time (less than 24 hours/week); (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) Lives with the claimant who is responsible for them; (6) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake; (7) Living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (8) Local council is not paying towards accommodation or maintenance. The claimant also meets residency requirements by normally living in the UK.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children, Alex (8) and Blake (14). Both children meet all the qualifying child requirements as they are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in apprenticeships. The claimant meets all claimant requirements: normally resident in the UK, responsible for both children (living with them and providing for all their needs), no other claimant exists for either child, both children live normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.
